explanation: GITHUB_TOKEN defaults to write-all in many repo configurations. GHA docs and Koishybayev et al. (2022) confirm 99.8% of workflows are overprivileged due to this omission.
justifiable: NOT justifiable unless org-level default read-only token policy is enforced (verify separately)

evidence: job 'delete_deploy_keys': runs-on: ubuntu-latest
explanation: The -latest runner label is redirected by GitHub to newer images on their own schedule. For C++ builds this silently changes compiler toolchain and system library versions. For Java it can change JDK availability. Pin to a specific version (e.g. ubuntu-22.04).
justifiable: POSSIBLY justifiable: Justifiable if workflow does not depend on specific system library or tool versions

evidence: Jobs missing timeout-minutes: delete_deploy_keys
explanation: GHA default is 360 minutes. A hung Maven/Gradle dependency resolution or C++ linker invocation will silently burn 6 hours of runner minutes. Set a realistic timeout that reflects actual expected job duration.
justifiable: POSSIBLY justifiable: Partially justifiable for very long but expected builds (e.g. full C++ compilation suites)

explanation: GITHUB_TOKEN defaults to write-all in many repo configurations. GHA docs and Koishybayev et al. (2022) confirm 99.8% of workflows are overprivileged due to this omission.
justifiable: NOT justifiable unless org-level default read-only token policy is enforced (verify separately)

evidence: uses: ad-m/github-push-action@master
explanation: Branch references are the most dangerous pinning — any push to that branch silently changes the code running in your workflow with no version signal or audit trail.
justifiable: NOT justifiable — no safe context for this pattern per GHA documentation

evidence: Jobs missing timeout-minutes: benchmarks
explanation: GHA default is 360 minutes. A hung Maven/Gradle dependency resolution or C++ linker invocation will silently burn 6 hours of runner minutes. Set a realistic timeout that reflects actual expected job duration.
justifiable: POSSIBLY justifiable: Partially justifiable for very long but expected builds (e.g. full C++ compilation suites)

rule_name: Privileged Trigger Exposing Secrets to Fork Code

evidence: Install commands found ({'pip install'}) but no caching step

explanation: A static cache key means the cache never invalidates when dependencies change. The workflow silently uses outdated packages. Fix: include hashFiles('**/requirements.txt'), hashFiles('**/pom.xml'), or hashFiles('**/CMakeLists.txt') in the cache key.
justifiable: POSSIBLY justifiable: Not justifiable — static keys silently serve stale dependencies

evidence: 32 steps with no reusable workflow or composite action reference
explanation: A large workflow with no reusable workflow calls suggests logic that could be abstracted. GHA recommends workflow_call and composite actions to prevent copy-paste duplication across workflow files in the same repo.
justifiable: POSSIBLY justifiable: Partially justifiable if workflows diverge intentionally across environments

evidence: No permissions: key found at workflow or job level
explanation: GITHUB_TOKEN defaults to write-all in many repo configurations. GHA docs and Koishybayev et al. (2022) confirm 99.8% of workflows are overprivileged due to this omission.
justifiable: NOT justifiable unless org-level default read-only token policy is enforced (verify separately)

evidence: job 'macos-built-distributions': runs-on: macos-latest; job 'pure-built-distributions': runs-on: ubuntu-latest; job 'source-distribution': runs-on: ubuntu-latest; job 'publish': runs-on: ubuntu-latest
explanation: The -latest runner label is redirected by GitHub to newer images on their own schedule. For C++ builds this silently changes compiler toolchain and system library versions. For Java it can change JDK availability. Pin to a specific version (e.g. ubuntu-22.04).
